attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man is brought in by his son with a 3-day history of a productive cough and pleuritic chest pain. His medical history includes chronic pancreatitis and recurrent episodes of aspiration pneumonia. He does not take any regular medications and does not see a General Practitioner regularly. He is admitted and started on appropriate empiric antibiotic therapy.

On examination, he appears malnourished but alert and oriented. He is febrile, tachycardic and the right lower lung field is dull to percussion with crackles on auscultation. There is no jaundice or ascites. A chest X-ray confirms right lower lobe pneumonia. He is admitted to hospital.

The next day he is seen stumbling on the ward and appears disoriented. He remains tachycardic, but other vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, a rhythmic, uncontrolled oscillation of both eyes on lateral gaze is noted.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
The triad of confusion, ataxia, and oculomotor dysfunction is characteristic of Wernicke encephalopathy, which is commonly triggered by infections. Horizontal nystagmus is the most common oculomotor finding, as seen in this case, followed by ophthalmoplegia. These signs reflect damage to the midbrain and the mamillary bodies. This is an acute complication of thiamine deficiency most commonly seen in patients with chronic alcoholism or severe malnourishment. This makes IV thiamine the most appropriate management.

In contrast, Korsakoff syndrome is a chronic neuropsychiatric condition that may follow Wernicke encephalopathy. Patients commonly present with confabulation, selective anterograde and retrograde amnesia.

Hypoglycemia is an important cause of altered mental status however it does not explain the horizontal nystagmus. More importantly, administration of IV glucose in thiamine-deficient patients may precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy. It is common practice to administer high-dose IV thiamine before glucose when treating these patients.

Alcohol withdrawal is an important differential diagnosis however it does not explain the horizontal nystagmus. Early alcohol withdrawal symptoms may be mild including sweating, anxiety, gastrointestinal upset, headache and palpitations. However, untreated alcohol withdrawal can lead to severe complications including seizures, and delirium tremens. Oral diazepam is used to treat alcohol withdrawal and to prevent these complications.

Oral lactulose decreases blood ammonia level, thereby making it a useful treatment in hepatic encephalopathy. This diagnosis is less likely due to the absence of history or signs of chronic liver disease. It also does not explain the finding of horizontal nystagmus.

IV magnesium may be appropriate if he is hypomagnesaemic. Magnesium is a cofactor that assists with utilisation of thiamine. However, IV thiamine remains the mainstay of treatment for Wernicke encephalopathy.
learningPoints: Wernicke encephalopathy presents with ataxia, confusion and oculomotor dysfunction (eg, nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia).
Treatment and prevention of Wernicke encephalopathy with high-dose IV thiamine is crucial to prevent progression to Korsakoff syndrome.
Glucose can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy in thiamine-deficient patients if given prior to thiamine replacement.
references: Coughlan JJ, Mross T, Wafer M, Liston R. Thiamine refractory Wernickes encephalopathy reversed with magnesium therapy. BMJ Case Rep. 2016 Oct 25;2016.
Latt N, Dore G. Thiamine in the treatment of Wernicke encephalopathy in patients with alcohol use disorders. Intern Med J. 2014 Sep;44(9):911–5.
Sinha S, Kataria A, Kolla BP, Thusius N, Loukianova LL. Wernicke Encephalopathy-Clinical Pearls. Mayo Clin Proc. 2019 Jun;94(6):1065–72.
